planting.note: Root semi-hardwood cuttings in summer or plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, average to fertile, slightly acidic to alkaline soil in partial shade to full sun in spring; Japanese pittosporum is extremely adaptable and one of the most widely planted ornamental shrubs in Gulf Coast landscapes.

directions: Root semi-hardwood cuttings in summer or plant container specimens in well-drained, average to fertile, slightly acidic to alkaline soil in partial shade to full sun; Japanese pittosporum is one of the most widely planted foundation and hedge shrubs in Gulf Coast and southern California landscapes, prized for its dense, glossy, dark-green, rounded foliage arranged in attractive whorls, powerful fragrance of orange blossom from the creamy-white flowers in spring, and extreme adaptability to heat, drought, salt spray, poor soil, and shade. The compact selection 'Wheeler's Dwarf' (2–3 feet) is extremely popular for formal hedging and low foundation plantings. 'Variegatum' (gray-green with white margins) provides lighter foliage contrast. The shrub tolerates hard pruning and can be shaped into formal hedges, trained as a small tree, or left to grow in its natural rounded form. An essential utility shrub for zone 8–11 gardens.
sources: Clemson Cooperative Extension Home & Garden Information Center

planting.note: Root semi-hardwood cuttings in summer or plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, average to poor, slightly acidic to alkaline soil in full sun to partial shade in spring or fall; thorny elaeagnus is extremely tough and adapts to a wide range of difficult conditions including drought, salt spray, and heavy soils.

directions: Root semi-hardwood cuttings or plant container specimens in well-drained, average to poor, slightly acidic to alkaline soil in full sun to partial shade; thorny elaeagnus is a large, vigorous, thorny, evergreen to semi-evergreen shrub from Asia with distinctive silvery-brown scales on the lower leaf surfaces that give the foliage an attractive metallic sheen. Small, intensely fragrant, white to cream-colored flowers appear in October–November — one of the few strongly scented fall-flowering shrubs — followed by reddish berries (edible but tart) in spring. It is extremely tough — among the most stress-tolerant shrubs for dry, salty, alkaline, or compacted soils and full shade — and fixes nitrogen through root nodules. Excellent for barrier hedges, windbreaks, and difficult dry or salt-spray sites in the Gulf South. 'Fruitlandii' (larger, more silver leaves) and 'Maculata' (golden-splashed variegation) are popular selections.
sources: Clemson Cooperative Extension Home & Garden Information Center
North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Root semi-hardwood cuttings in summer or plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average soil in full sun to partial shade in spring; pyracantha requires full sun for best berry production — plants in shade produce fewer fruits.

directions: Root semi-hardwood cuttings or plant container specimens in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average soil in full sun to partial shade; firethorn is an evergreen thorny shrub that earns its place in the landscape through a spectacular fall and winter display of clustered, brilliant orange, red, or yellow berries that cover the shrub in such abundance they literally hide the foliage — providing one of the most eye-catching ornamental fruit displays available in temperate gardens. White flower clusters in spring attract bees. Excellent as an espalier against a wall or fence, as a barrier hedge (the thorns are fierce), or as a freestanding specimen. Cedar waxwings, robins, and mockingbirds flock to the berries. 'Mohave' (orange-red, disease-resistant), 'Lalandei' (orange, vigorous), and 'Teton' (yellow-orange, upright) are widely grown. Berries are mildly toxic to humans; do not eat.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database
Clemson Cooperative Extension Home & Garden Information Center

planting.note: Root semi-hardwood cuttings in summer or plant container-grown specimens in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to alkaline soil in partial shade to full sun in spring or fall; laurustinus is one of the most adaptable and shade-tolerant of all evergreen viburnums.

directions: Root semi-hardwood cuttings or plant container specimens in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to alkaline soil in partial shade to full sun; laurustinus is a Mediterranean evergreen viburnum that provides exceptional winter interest — the flower buds, held in flat-topped clusters, open from pink buds to white flowers from late winter through spring, providing bloom when nearly nothing else flowers in the garden. After bloom, the fruits pass through a striking metallic turquoise-blue phase before ripening to black, providing a second season of ornamental value. It is one of the most shade-tolerant and adaptable evergreen viburnums, performing well in partial shade, alkaline soil, and coastal conditions. 'Eve Price' (compact, deep-pink buds), 'Spring Bouquet' (compact, freely flowering), and 'Lisarose' (rich pink) are excellent cultivar selections.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Root semi-hardwood cuttings in late summer or plant container-grown specimens in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade in spring; Chinese mahonia is more shade-tolerant and less cold-hardy than Oregon grape (M. aquifolium).

directions: Root semi-hardwood cuttings in late summer or plant container specimens in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; Chinese mahonia is a more refined, less aggressive alternative to Oregon grape (M. aquifolium) for warm-climate shade gardens, producing an upright, narrow form with elegant pinnate leaves bearing multiple narrow, deeply toothed, dark-green, glossy leaflets and dense clusters of fragrant yellow flowers in fall followed by small, blueberry-like, powdery-blue fruits. It is specifically more shade-tolerant and heat-tolerant than the popular Oregon grape. The evergreen foliage provides year-round structure in the darkest parts of the shade garden. Valuable for winter interest with both flowers and persistent fruits. Excellent in formal shaded landscapes and Japanese-inspired gardens.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database
Clemson Cooperative Extension Home & Garden Information Center
